blocks.content: At some point — Ethan registered it vaguely, the way you register something done skillfully — Mr. Alvarez reappeared at the table.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Nathan.
Not a question. A summons dressed as one.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: Yes?

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: The supplier sent updated calibration instructions for the espresso machine. I wonder if you could look at them with me. You seem like a man who understands calibration.

Nathan looked at the machine behind the counter, which was operating normally, producing coffee at precisely the same rate it always had. He looked at Ethan, who gave him nothing. He looked at Cassandra, who was looking at her cup with the expression of someone trying not to smile.

blocks.speaker: Nathan
blocks.content: It looks... fine to me.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: It is a subtle matter. Come.

Nathan stood with the manner of a man who understood that something was happening but couldn't quite name it, and followed Mr. Alvarez behind the counter. From where Ethan sat, he could watch Nathan gesturing at the machine with earnest uncertainty, and Mr. Alvarez listening with the patient expression of someone who has achieved exactly what he intended.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Did he just—

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Yes.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Deliberately?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Always.
She looked at the counter for a moment, then back at the table.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: He did that for us.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: He does it when he thinks two people need five minutes without an audience. I've seen it once before. He's usually right about when it's necessary.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Was he right that time?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: The couple he did it for got engaged eight months later.
She looked at him.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: That's a lot of pressure to put on a calibration conversation.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: He has a high success rate. He can afford the confidence.
A beat of quiet. Easy, not empty.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I wanted to tell you something. Without Nathan hearing it, because he would carry it around and reference it at specific moments for years. I wanted to say it while it was still close.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Then say it.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Yesterday was the best day I've had in a long time. Not the most dramatic. Not the most eventful. Just the best.
She was quiet for a moment.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You didn't have to wait for him to leave to say that.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I know. I wanted to say it right.
She reached across the table and touched the back of his hand — not taking it, just touching it, the way you make contact with something to confirm it's real.

Behind the counter, Nathan was nodding with apparent conviction about the calibration settings. Mr. Alvarez caught Ethan's eye over his shoulder. Something passed between them — quiet, certain, the look of a man who has been right about a thing and finds it unremarkable.
